canonical_definition: Across the provided literature, Principal Component Analysis (PCA) is consistently described as a dimensionality-reduction technique, though its specific applications vary. One use is to analyze a model's internal states by applying PCA to "residual stream activations" to visualize the "geometric structure of numerical representations across model layers" (RethinkMCTS: Refining Erroneous Thoughts inMonteCarlo Tree Search for Code Generation). Another application treats PCA as a "lens function" for projecting embeddings into a low-dimensional space, a process which "emphasizes directions of high variance" and highlights "global variance" (Does Acceleration Cause Hidden Instability in Vision Language Models? Uncovering Instance-Level Divergence Through a Large-Scale Empirical Study). A third documented use is for analyzing behavioral data, where it serves to "cluster co-occurring follow-up types into prototypical collaboration behaviors" (TORSO: Template-Oriented Reasoning Towards General Tasks). In all these cases, PCA is operationalized as an analytical tool to make high-dimensional data—whether from model internals, embeddings, or interaction logs—more interpretable by revealing underlying patterns.
papers: Adaptively profiling models with task elicitation
Does Acceleration Cause Hidden Instability in Vision Language Models? Uncovering Instance-Level Divergence Through a Large-Scale Empirical Study
Finding yourMUSE: Mining Unexpected Solutions Engine
Label Set Optimization via Activation Distribution Kurtosis for Zero-Shot Classification with Generative Models
RethinkMCTS: Refining Erroneous Thoughts inMonteCarlo Tree Search for Code Generation
SABER: Uncovering Vulnerabilities in Safety Alignment via Cross-Layer Residual Connection
Tailoring Table Retrieval from a Field-aware Hybrid Matching Perspective
